title: How to Get on a Building's Broker List
dek: Most luxury buildings keep some version of a broker list: the agents they'll co-op with, notify about availability, or send overflow renters to. Getting on it is rarely a formal application. It's a reputation you install, deliberately. Here's the sequence.
heroAlt: How to Get on a Building's Broker List

blocks.text: Step 1: walk in like a professional

blocks.html: Tour the building as an agent, openly. Introduce yourself to the leasing team, ask for a card, ask how they prefer to work with outside agents. Agents will spend hundreds on ads before they'll spend an afternoon walking into ten lobbies, which is exactly why walking in works.

blocks.text: The most underused tool in real estate is the front door.

blocks.html: Not "can you send me leads." Ask: how do you like showings scheduled? What does your application require? Who should I keep in touch with about availability? Operational questions signal you intend to bring business, not extract favors.

Nothing installs you on the list like one flawless transaction: a qualified renter, documents ready, application same-day, lease signed. One clean deal outweighs a year of coffee drop-ins. Prioritize getting a real client through your top target buildings early.
Follow the building and its management company online, engage professionally, and keep your twice-monthly touch rhythm. When a slot opens on their radar, familiar and reliable wins it.
Once you're in the flow, guard it. Never bring an unvetted client, never no-show, never argue commission after the fact. Buildings quietly remove agents far faster than they add them.

The broker list is the compounding asset of this business: it's how deals start arriving without you hunting them. The rest of the machine is in my free ebook Luxury Leases, Real Profits. Better agents win.
